title: Janqua
text:
Overview
Janqua is a Quarto extension for evaluating Jank code blocks in documents.
Write Jank code in .qmd files and get live results — code output, inline SVG, Plotly charts, markdown tables, and more. Uses the Kindly convention for rendering, compatible with the Clojure ecosystem.

title: Janqua
text:
What is Quarto?
Quarto is an open-source publishing system for technical documents. You write .qmd (Quarto Markdown) files mixing prose and code, and Quarto renders them to HTML, PDF, slides, and other formats.
Quarto supports several project types:

Single documents — a standalone .qmd file rendered to HTML
Websites — multiple pages with navigation, like a documentation site
Books — chapters rendered as a navigable book (like this one)

title: Janqua
text:
What is Jank?
Jank is a Clojure dialect that compiles to native code via C++. It aims for full Clojure compatibility with the performance of a native runtime. Jank includes an nREPL server, which Janqua uses to evaluate code interactively during document rendering.

title: Janqua
text:
Clojure ecosystem connection
Clay is a Clojure tool for data visualization and literate programming. Clay generates Quarto markdown from Clojure namespaces, then uses Quarto as a rendering engine to produce HTML pages, books, and slideshows. Clojure Civitas, the community blog, is built this way.
Janqua brings a similar experience to Jank: you write Jank code directly in .qmd files, and the Quarto filter evaluates it during rendering. Both Janqua and Clay use the Kindly convention for rendering annotations, so code patterns are transferable between the two ecosystems.

title: Janqua
text:
How it works

You write a .qmd file with {.jank} code blocks
When Quarto renders the document, the Janqua filter intercepts these blocks
Each block is sent to a Jank nREPL server for evaluation
Results are inserted into the document — as code output, HTML, or markdown — based on Kindly annotations

^:kind/hiccup
[:div {:style "color: coral; font-size: 24px;"} "Hello from Jank!"]
The Jank process starts automatically on first render and stays running for fast re-evaluation. See Getting Started to try it out, or jump to the Live Demo to see evaluated examples.

title: Janqua
text:
Future goals

Clojure Civitas integration — enabling community members to publish Jank posts on the blog
Broader Kindly support — more visualization kinds (tables, Vega-Lite, images, etc.)
Easier setup — reducing the number of prerequisites and simplifying installation
Clay support for Jank — a namespace-as-a-notebook experience, where Jank source files render as documents without writing .qmd by hand
Jupyter support — a Jank kernel for Jupyter notebooks, enabling interactive Jank development in JupyterLab and other Jupyter-compatible environments

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.1 Prerequisites
Verify everything is available:

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
Quarto — the publishing system
Jank — the Jank compiler and REPL
Babashka — fast Clojure scripting runtime
bbin — tool installer for Babashka
clj-nrepl-eval — nREPL client (install via bbin):

bbin install io.github.bhauman/clojure-mcp-light

quarto --version
jank --version
bb --version
clj-nrepl-eval --help

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.2 Create a project
mkdir my-jank-doc
cd my-jank-doc

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.3 Install the extension
Run this inside your project directory — it creates an _extensions/ folder there:
quarto add scicloj/janqua

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.4 Write a document
Create a file called hello.qmd:
---
title: "Hello Jank"
filters:
  - jank
---

```{.jank}
(+ 1 2 3)
```

```{.jank}
^:kind/hiccup
[:div {:style "color: coral; font-size: 24px;"} "Hello from Jank!"]
```
Note the {.jank} syntax — the dot prefix is required (it’s Pandoc class syntax). The filters: [jank] in the frontmatter tells Quarto to use the Janqua filter.

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.5 Render it
quarto render hello.qmd
This starts a Jank nREPL server (if one isn’t already running), evaluates the code blocks, and produces hello.html.
For a live-reloading workflow, use quarto preview instead — it re-renders automatically each time you save:
quarto preview hello.qmd
This opens a browser with live preview. Edit your .qmd, save, and the page refreshes with updated results. Press Ctrl-C to stop.

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.6 Managing the Jank process
The Jank nREPL server starts on the first render and stays running for fast re-evaluation. You can manage it with the lifecycle script:
_extensions/jank/jank-lifecycle.sh status   # check if running
_extensions/jank/jank-lifecycle.sh start    # start manually
_extensions/jank/jank-lifecycle.sh stop     # stop when done
Stop the server when you’re done working to free resources.

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.7 Publishing to GitHub Pages
If your project is a Quarto book or website, you can publish it to GitHub Pages directly from your project directory:
quarto publish gh-pages
This renders the project and pushes the output to the gh-pages branch. GitHub Pages then serves it as a website. On first use, Quarto will create the branch and configure it for you.
See the Quarto publishing guide for more options, including custom domains and GitHub Actions automation.

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.8 Next steps

Learn about Kindly annotations to render HTML, SVG, charts, and tables
See the Live Demo for evaluated examples

title: 2  Kindly Annotations
text:
2.1 ^:kind/hiccup — HTML from data
Kindly is a convention from the Clojure ecosystem for annotating values with rendering intent. Janqua supports Kindly metadata, so the same code patterns work in both Jank+Quarto and Clojure+Clay.
Without an annotation, results display as code output. Add Kindly metadata to control how a value is rendered.
Converts a hiccup vector to HTML. Hiccup represents HTML as nested Clojure vectors:
The vector [:tag {:attr "value"} content] becomes <tag attr="value">content</tag>.

title: 2  Kindly Annotations
2  Kindly Annotations
text:
2.2 ^:kind/html — raw HTML
Renders a string as raw HTML. Since strings can’t hold metadata in Clojure, wrap the expression in a vector:
```{.jank}
^:kind/html
[(str "<b>bold</b> and <em>italic</em>")]
```
The vector is automatically unwrapped — Janqua extracts the first element for rendering.

title: 2  Kindly Annotations
text:
2.3 ^:kind/md — markdown
Renders a string as markdown. Same wrapping rule as kind/html:
```{.jank}
^:kind/md
[(str "| a | b |\n| --- | --- |\n| 1 | 2 |")]
```

title: 2  Kindly Annotations
text:
2.4 ^:kind/hidden — suppress output
Evaluates the code but hides the result. The code itself is still shown. Useful for setup like defining helper functions:
```{.jank}
^:kind/hidden
[(def my-data [1 2 3])]
```

title: 2  Kindly Annotations
text:
2.5 Long form
The shorthand ^:kind/hiccup is equivalent to the explicit ^{:kindly/kind :kind/hiccup}:
```{.jank}
^{:kindly/kind :kind/hiccup}
[:div "hello"]
```
Use whichever you prefer. The shorthand is more concise; the long form is explicit.

title: 2  Kindly Annotations
text:
2.6 Annotating in library functions
The ^:kind/... reader syntax attaches metadata at the call site. But metadata can also be attached programmatically with with-meta or vary-meta, which means library functions can annotate their return values. The caller doesn’t need to know about the rendering — the function handles it.
For example, a bar-chart function could generate a Plotly plot and tag it as HTML:
(defn bar-chart [labels values]
  (let [html (str "<div id='chart'></div>"
                  "<script>Plotly.newPlot('chart', [{x: "
                  (pr-str labels) ", y: " (pr-str values)
                  ", type: 'bar'}])</script>")]
    (with-meta [html] {:kindly/kind :kind/html})))
Then in a code block, simply calling the function renders the chart — no annotation needed:
```{.jank}
(bar-chart ["a" "b" "c"] [10 20 15])
```
This is the key idea behind Kindly: rendering intent lives in the data, not in the document markup. Libraries can provide visualization functions that “just work” in any Kindly-aware tool — Janqua, Clay, or others.

title: 2  Kindly Annotations
text:
2.7 Why wrap strings in vectors?
In Clojure (and Jank), metadata can only be attached to collections — vectors, maps, sets — not to primitive types like strings or numbers. So ^:kind/html "hello" won’t work.
The convention is to wrap the value in a vector:
^:kind/html ["<b>hello</b>"]
Janqua automatically unwraps the vector for kind/html and kind/md, extracting the first element for rendering.

title: 2  Kindly Annotations
text:
2.8 Code block attributes
These attributes go on the code fence and control display independently of Kindly:



Attribute
Effect




echo=false
Hide the source code, show only the result


eval=false
Show code without evaluating it



```{.jank echo=false}
^:kind/hiccup
[:div "Only the rendered HTML appears — no source code shown"]
```

title: 2  Kindly Annotations
text:
2.9 Summary



Kind
Value type
Renders as




^:kind/hiccup
vector (hiccup)
HTML


^:kind/html
[string]
raw HTML


^:kind/md
[string]
markdown


^:kind/hidden
any
nothing (evaluates silently)


(none)
any
code output

title: 3  How It Works
text:
3.1 Architecture overview
This chapter explains the internals of Janqua — how your {.jank} code blocks turn into rendered output.
Janqua is a Pandoc Lua filter. When Quarto renders a .qmd file, it calls Pandoc under the hood. Pandoc reads the document, builds an abstract syntax tree (AST), and then runs any filters specified in the frontmatter. The Janqua filter intercepts code blocks marked with the .jank class, evaluates them via a Jank nREPL server, and replaces them with the appropriate output.
The filter has two phases, executed in order:

title: 3  How It Works
text:
┌─────────┐     ┌────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌──────────┐
│ .qmd    │────▶│ Pandoc  │────▶│ Lua filter  │────▶│ HTML/PDF │
│ file    │     │ (parse) │     │ (eval Jank) │     │ output   │
└─────────┘     └────────┘     └──────┬──────┘     └──────────┘
                                      │
                                      ▼
                               ┌──────────────┐
                               │ Jank nREPL   │
                               │ server       │
                               └──────────────┘


Meta — reads document metadata and resolves the Jank nREPL port
CodeBlock — processes each {.jank} code block

title: 3  How It Works
text:
3.2 Port discovery
The filter needs to find a running Jank nREPL server. It tries several sources in order, validating that each candidate port is actually reachable before using it:

Frontmatter — an explicit jank.port value in the document metadata
PID + port files — .jank-pid and .jank-nrepl-port written by the lifecycle script
Environment variable — JANK_PORT
Process scan — searches for a jank process listening on a TCP port (via lsof or ss)
Auto-start — if nothing is found, the filter launches jank repl via the lifecycle script and waits for it to be ready

This chain means zero configuration for the common case: just run quarto render and the filter handles the rest.

title: 3  How It Works
text:
3.3 Process management
Jank’s REPL exits when its standard input closes. To keep it alive across renders, the lifecycle script (jank-lifecycle.sh) pipes tail -f /dev/null into the REPL’s stdin — an infinite stream of nothing that keeps the process running.
The lifecycle script manages three files in the project root:



File
Purpose




.jank-pid
PID of the running Jank process


.jank-nrepl-port
The nREPL port Jank is listening on


.jank-repl.log
Jank’s stdout/stderr output



A background monitor watches the Jank process and cleans up these files if it crashes.
The stop command verifies the PID still belongs to a Jank process before sending a kill signal — this prevents accidentally killing an unrelated process that reused the PID.

title: 3  How It Works
text:
3.4 Code evaluation
Each {.jank} code block goes through these steps:

3.4.1 1. Kindly wrapping
The user’s code is wrapped in a function call that captures the result and its metadata:
;; User writes:
^:kind/hiccup [:div "hello"]

;; Filter sends to Jank:
((fn [v]
   ((fn [m]
      ((fn [kind]
         {:janqua/kind kind
          :janqua/value (pr-str v)})
       (when m (or (:kindly/kind m) ...))))
    (meta v)))
 (do ^:kind/hiccup [:div "hello"]))
The wrapper uses nested fn calls rather than let bindings — this works around a Jank bug where reader-attached metadata (like ^:kind/hiccup) is lost through let bindings.


3.4.2 2. nREPL communication
The wrapped code is sent to Jank via clj-nrepl-eval, a Babashka-based nREPL client. The filter parses the response to extract:

stdout — anything printed during evaluation
value — the return value (serialized via pr-str)

Session state persists between evaluations — defs and requires carry forward, so earlier code blocks can define functions used by later ones.


3.4.3 3. Kind detection
The filter examines the :janqua/kind field from the wrapper response:







Kind
Output




:kind/hiccup
Hiccup data is converted to HTML via a helper function defined in the Jank session


:kind/html
String is inserted as raw HTML


:kind/md or :kind/markdown
String is parsed as Markdown and inserted


:kind/hidden
Output is suppressed (code still evaluates)


(none)
Value is shown as a code block



For kind/html and kind/md, strings can’t hold metadata directly (they’re primitives in Jank). The convention is to wrap them in a vector: ^:kind/html ["<b>bold</b>"] — the filter auto-unwraps the first element.


3.4.4 4. Hiccup rendering
When a value has :kind/hiccup metadata, the filter calls janqua-hiccup->html — a function bootstrapped in the Jank session on first use. This converts Clojure-style hiccup data structures into HTML strings:
[:div {:class "box"} [:span "hi"]]
;; becomes:
"<div class=\"box\"><span>hi</span></div>"
This runs inside Jank (not in Lua), so it has access to the full Clojure data model.

title: 3  How It Works
text:
3.5 Code block attributes
The filter respects several attributes on {.jank} blocks:



Attribute
Effect




output=html
Force HTML output (override Kindly)


output=markdown
Force Markdown output


output=hidden
Suppress both code and output


echo=false
Hide the source code, show only results


eval=false
Show code without evaluating



Kindly metadata on the result takes precedence over output= when both are present.

title: 3  How It Works
text:
3.6 Bootstrap sequence
On the first evaluation in a document, the filter:

Resolves the nREPL port (auto-starting Jank if needed)
Sends the janqua-hiccup->html function definition to the Jank session
Begins processing code blocks in document order

Subsequent renders reuse the same Jank process, so startup cost is only paid once.

title: 4  Live Demo
text:
4.1 Basics
This page contains live Jank code — each block is evaluated during rendering, and the results appear below.

title: 4  Live Demo
text:
(+ 1 2 3)

6

(str "Hello from " "Jank!")

"Hello from Jank!"

(defn square [x] (* x x))

#'user/square

(map square [1 2 3 4 5])

(1 4 9 16 25)

title: 4  Live Demo
text:
4.2 Stdout
Printed output appears alongside the return value:
(do (println "computing...") (* 6 7))

computing...
42

title: 4  Live Demo
text:
4.3 Setup helpers
^:kind/hidden
[(defn to-json [v]
   (cond
     (nil? v) "null"
     (true? v) "true"
     (false? v) "false"
     (string? v) (str "\"" v "\"")
     (keyword? v) (str "\"" (name v) "\"")
     (number? v) (str v)
     (vector? v) (str "[" (clojure.string/join ", " (map to-json v)) "]")
     (seq? v) (str "[" (clojure.string/join ", " (map to-json v)) "]")
     (map? v) (str "{"
                (clojure.string/join ", "
                  (map (fn [[k val]]
                         (str (to-json k) ": " (to-json val)))
                       v))
                "}")
     :else (str v)))]

title: 4  Live Demo
text:
4.4 Hiccup
Annotate a vector with ^:kind/hiccup to render it as HTML:
^:kind/hiccup
[:svg {:width "300" :height "200" :xmlns "http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"}
  [:rect {:width "300" :height "200" :fill "#f0f0f0" :rx "10"}]
  [:circle {:cx "80" :cy "100" :r "50" :fill "steelblue"}]
  [:circle {:cx "160" :cy "100" :r "40" :fill "coral"}]
  [:circle {:cx "230" :cy "100" :r "30" :fill "mediumseagreen"}]
  [:text {:x "150" :y "180" :text-anchor "middle" :font-size "14" :fill "#333"}
    "Generated by Jank"]]
Generated by Jank
The long form ^{:kindly/kind :kind/hiccup} works too:
^{:kindly/kind :kind/hiccup}
[:div {:style "padding:12px; background:#f0f0f0; border-radius:8px;"}
  [:b "Hello"] " from " [:em "Kindly"] "!"]
Hello from Kindly!

title: 4  Live Demo
text:
4.5 HTML
Use ^:kind/html to render a computed string as raw HTML. Since strings can’t hold metadata, wrap the expression in a vector:
^:kind/html
[(str "<div style='padding:8px; border:2px solid #4a90d9; border-radius:8px;'>"
      "<b>Computed HTML</b> via <code>kind/html</code>"
      "</div>")]
Computed HTML via kind/html

title: 4  Live Demo
text:
4.6 Plotly chart
^:kind/html
[(let [xs (vec (map #(* 0.5 %) (range 20)))
       ys (vec (map #(+ (* % %) (* -0.5 %)) xs))]
   (str "<div id=\"plot1\"></div>"
        "<script src=\"https://cdn.plot.ly/plotly-2.35.0.min.js\"></script>"
        "<script>Plotly.newPlot('plot1', "
        (to-json {:data [{:x xs :y ys
                          :type "scatter" :mode "lines+markers"
                          :name "y = x² - 0.5x"}]
                  :layout {:title "Quadratic from Jank"
                           :xaxis {:title "x"}
                           :yaxis {:title "y"}}})
        ");</script>"))]

title: 4  Live Demo
text:
4.7 Markdown table
Use ^:kind/md to render a string as markdown:
^:kind/md
[(let [headers ["x" "x²" "x³"]
       rows (map (fn [x] [(str x) (str (* x x)) (str (* x x x))]) (range 1 8))
       header-line (str "| " (clojure.string/join " | " headers) " |")
       sep-line (str "| " (clojure.string/join " | " (map (fn [_] "---") headers)) " |")
       row-lines (map (fn [row] (str "| " (clojure.string/join " | " row) " |")) rows)]
   (clojure.string/join "\n" (concat [header-line sep-line] row-lines)))]



x
x²
x³




1
1
1


2
4
8


3
9
27


4
16
64


5
25
125


6
36
216


7
49
343

title: 4  Live Demo
text:
4.8 LaTeX math
Since kind/md output is processed as markdown, LaTeX math works via $$...$$ syntax. Quarto renders it with MathJax:
^:kind/md
[(str "$$\\sum_{k=1}^{n} k = \\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$")]
\[\sum_{k=1}^{n} k = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\]
You can also build formulas from computed values:
^:kind/md
[(let [n 10
       result (/ (* n (+ n 1)) 2)]
   (str "For $n = " n "$:\n\n"
        "$$\\sum_{k=1}^{" n "} k = \\frac{" n " \\cdot " (+ n 1) "}{2} = " result "$$"))]
For \(n = 10\):
\[\sum_{k=1}^{10} k = \frac{10 \cdot 11}{2} = 55\]

title: 4  Live Demo
text:
4.9 Annotating in library functions
Metadata doesn’t have to be in the code block — functions can attach it to their return values with with-meta. Define a bar-chart function that produces a Plotly chart and tags it as :kind/html:
^:kind/hidden
[(defn bar-chart [labels values]
   (with-meta
     [(str "<div id='bar1'></div>"
           "<script>Plotly.newPlot('bar1', "
           (to-json {:data [{:x labels :y values :type "bar"}]
                     :layout {:title "Bar Chart"}})
           ");</script>")]
     {:kindly/kind :kind/html}))]
Now just call the function — no annotation needed at the call site:
(bar-chart ["apples" "bananas" "cherries"] [12 19 7])
